narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, three key elements stood out as having the strongest sway on this outcome. Your LSAT score was a major positive force, performing above the average for students who passed the bar (around 37), which pulled the prediction toward success and helped keep the failure risk from being even higher. On the flip side, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 was the biggest drag, far below the typical 6 seen among bar passers, signaling to the model a concerning dip in later academic performance that pushed strongly toward a failure prediction. Your upper-middle family income also nudged things positively, aligning with the most common bracket (about 45%) among successful students, offering some reassurance compared to lower-income profiles. Overall, while your LSAT and income provided helpful boosts, the low Year 3 decile outweighed them, tipping the balance against admission when stacked against profiles of accepted students who generally show steadier grade progress. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the 3.24 average for passers, and full-time enrollment plans played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's read based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to strengthen that asset further, or focus on boosting late-stage grades through targeted prep or additional undergrad coursework to improve your decile standing. You might also explore part-time programs or gap-year experiences that have helped similar students build stronger profiles. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized feedback, and keep pursuing your goals—many have turned predictions like this around with strategic next steps.
